records.ety: [L. trochus an iron hoop, Gr. <?/ a wheel, fr. <?/ to run. See Trochee, and cf. Truckle, v. i.]

defs: A small wheel, as of a vehicle; specifically (Ord.), a small strong wheel, as of wood or iron, for a gun carriage.
A low, wheeled vehicle or barrow for carrying goods, stone, and other heavy articles.
quotes: Goods were conveyed about the town almost exclusively in trucks drawn by dogs.

defs: A swiveling carriage, consisting of a frame with one or more pairs of wheels and the necessary boxes, springs, etc., to carry and guide one end of a locomotive or a car; -- sometimes called bogie in England. Trucks usually have four or six wheels.
A small wooden cap at the summit of a flagstaff or a masthead, having holes in it for reeving halyards through.
A small piece of wood, usually cylindrical or disk-shaped, used for various purposes.
A frame on low wheels or rollers; -- used for various purposes, as for a movable support for heavy bodies.
a motorized vehicle larger than an automobile with a compartment in front for the driver, behind which is a separate compartment for freight;
such a vehicle with an inflexible body.
A vehicle with a short body and a support for attaching a trailer; -- also called a tractor{4}.
the combination of tractor and trailer, also called a tractor-trailer (a form of articulated vehicle); it is a common form of truck, and is used primarily for hauling freight on a highway.
a tractor with more than one trailer attached in a series. In Australia, often referred to as a road train.
To transport on a truck or trucks.

records.ety: [OE. trukken,F. troquer; akin to Sp. & Pg. trocar; of uncertain origin.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Trucked (?); p. pr. & vb. n. trucking.]

defs: To exchange; to give in exchange; to barter; as, to truck knives for gold dust.
quotes: We will begin by supposing the international trade to be in form, what it always is in reality, an actual trucking of one commodity against another.

defs: To exchange commodities; to barter; to trade; to deal.
quotes: A master of a ship, who deceived them under color of trucking with them.
Despotism itself is obliged to truck and huckster.
To truck and higgle for a private good.

defs: Commodities appropriate for barter, or for small trade; small commodities; esp., in the United States, garden vegetables raised for the market.
The practice of paying wages in goods instead of money; -- called also truck system.
